macro_segments.title: The Story of Elisha ben Abuyah (Acher): His Daughter's Plea and the Defense of His Honor

micro_segments.title: Acher's Daughter Comes to Rabbi Seeking Support
Rabbi Questions Whether Acher's Descendants Still Exist, Citing Verse About No Remnant
Acher's Daughter Responds: Remember His Torah, Not His Deeds; Fire Defends His Honor
Rabbi's Conclusion: Those Who Praise Torah Deserve Honor; Ambivalence Toward Acher

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Meir Learning from Acher: Scriptural Justification and the Adult vs. Child Distinction

micro_segments.title: Question: How Could Rabbi Meir Learn Torah from Acher? The Verse About Seeking Torah from Angels
Rabbi Meir's Scriptural Defense: Incline Your Ear to the Sages, But Seek My Intent (Not Theirs)
Alternative Verse: Listen and Forget Your Nation and Father's House (Forget Their Deeds)
Reconciling Contradictory Verses: Adult vs. Child—A Discerning Adult Can Be Selective

macro_segments.title: The Parable of the Date and the Pomegranate: Selective Learning and the Preservation of Torah

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Meir Ate the Date and Discarded the Pit: The Image of Being Selective
Rava's Parable: The Nut—Even If the Husk Is Disgusting, the Nut Inside Is Preserved
Application: A Torah Scholar Who Has Gone Astray Still Preserves Torah Within
Rabbeinu Chananel's Objection and Elijah's Statement: Even If Not Taught Above, Torah Is Better from Observant Sources

macro_segments.title: The Parable of the Date and the Pomegranate: Selective Learning and the Preservation of Torah (continued)

micro_segments.title: Heavenly Response: God No Longer Teaches Rabbi Meir's Teachings Because He Learned from Acher

macro_segments.title: The Pomegranate Seeds Incident: God Quotes Rabbi Meir in Heaven and Teaches Empathy for Even the Wicked

micro_segments.title: Elijah Tells Rabba bar Shila What God Is Doing: Teaching the Teachings of All Rabbis
Rabba bar Shila's Challenge: Rabbi Meir Learned from Acher, So Why Teach His Teachings?
Elijah Responds: Now That Meir Found a Pomegranate and Ate Seeds, God Will Quote Him
Rabbi Meir's Teaching in Heaven: When a Person Suffers, God Suffers; Greater for the Righteous (Allusion to Acher)

macro_segments.title: The Testimony of Samuel and Rav Yehuda: Character, Corruption, and the Greek Song

micro_segments.title: Samuel Finds Rav Yehuda Weeping Over the Loss of Great Scholars; Question About How Such Greatness Can Fall
Rav Yehuda's Lament: Great Sages Like Doeg and Achitofel Knew 300 Questions Yet Still Lost Their Share in the World to Come
Rav Yehuda's Worry: If Such Great Scholars Could Fall, What Hope for Us?
Samuel's Answer: Corruption Was in Their Heart from the Beginning (tinah hayita belibam)
The Greek Song Never Ceased from Acher's Mouth: Cultural Attraction as Corrosive Force
Books of Minim Fell from Acher's Lap: Evidence of Hidden Intellectual and Cultural Seduction

macro_segments.title: The Wool and Dye Parable: Why Torah Cannot Always Transform a Corrupted Character

micro_segments.title: Nimus the Weaver Asks Rabbi Meir: Does All Wool Come Out Well-Dyed?
Rabbi Meir's Answer: Clean Wool (Good Character) Holds the Dye; Unclean Wool (Bad Character) Does Not
Implication: Torah's Transformative Power Depends on the Raw Material of a Person's Character

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Akiva's Ascent to Pardes: The Righteous Sage Who Came Up and Down in Peace

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Akiva Went Up in Peace and Came Down in Peace; Verse: Draw Me After You and I Will Run
Angels Tried to Push Akiva Away, But God Said He Is Fit to Use My Honor
Rabbeinu Chananel's Reading: Fit to Gaze Upon God's Glory (Vision of the Divine Presence)

macro_segments.title: Distinguishing God from the Divine Retinue: How Rabbi Akiva Avoided Acher's Mistake

micro_segments.title: The Sign (Ot) in God's Myriad: God Is Distinct from All the Angels
Rabbi Abahu: God Is an Example (Dugma) Among the Myriads
Resh Lakish: God Is the Master (Adon) of His Hosts
Rabbi Chiya bar Abba Quoting Rabbi Yochanan: The Verse About Wind, Earthquake, and Fire—God Is Beyond Them
The Word Acher in That Verse: Allusion to Acher's Confusion of Metatron with Divinity

macro_segments.title: Six Things About Shedim and Six Things About Humans: The Hybrid Nature of Humanity

micro_segments.title: Three Things About Shedim Like Angels: Wings, Flight, Knowledge of Future (via Heavenly Voice)
Three Things About Shedim Like Humans: Eating, Drinking, Procreation, and Death
Three Things About Humans Like Angels: Intellect, Upright Posture, Spoken Language (Hebrew)
Three Things About Humans Like Animals: Eating, Drinking, Procreation, Excretion, and Death
Conclusion: The Paradox of Human Nature—Bridging the Divine and the Physical

macro_segments.title: Preserving God's Honor: What Not to Investigate—The Unknowable Before, Above, Below, and Future

micro_segments.title: The Four Things One Must Not Look At: What Is Above, Below, Behind (Future), and Before (Origin)
The Question: Why Is the Past (What Was) Permissible, While the Other Three Are Not?
Rabbi Yochanan bar Eleazar's Parable: The King's Castle Built on a Dung Heap—Don't Mention the Origins
Application: God Made the World from Tohu v'Vohu; Questioning Origins Disrespects God's Honor

macro_segments.title: The Rainbow, the King, and the Kohanim: Three Things That Dim the Eyes When Gazed Upon

micro_segments.title: Gazing Upon the Rainbow: It Represents the Appearance of God's Glory
Gazing Upon a King: He Has Divine Glory Bestowed Upon Him
Gazing Upon Kohanim in the Sanctuary: Their Fingers Carry the Divine Presence When Blessing Israel
Rabbeinu Chananel: The Problem Is Thinking the Rainbow Is God's Glory (When It Is Not)
Kabbalistic Reading: The Rainbow as a Mystical Symbol (Discussion of Reverence and Concealment)

macro_segments.title: Sinning in Private and Public: The Theological Problem and the Hierarchy of Transgressions

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yosef: Sinning in Private Shows Disrespect for God's Presence (Pushing Away God's Footstool)
Rabbi Isaac: One Who Sins in Private Acts as if God Cannot See Him
Rabbi Illai the Elder's Lenient View: If Desire Overwhelms You, Go Where Unknown, Dress in Black, Sin Privately
Reconciliation: The Difference Is Whether One Can Control the Desire (Matikif Lei Yetzro vs. Lo Matikif Lei Yetzro)
Conclusion: If You Must Sin, Better Private Than Public; If You Can Avoid, Do Not Sin At All
Rishonim's Objection: The Passage Should Not Be Read to License Private Sin; Rather, It Discourages Through Humility

macro_segments.title: Do Not Believe Your Evil Inclination: The Final Warnings and the Testimony Against Secret Deeds

micro_segments.title: Verse Interpretation: Do Not Believe in the Evil (Yetzer Hara), Do Not Trust in the Dignitary (God)
The Yetzer Hara's False Argument: Go Ahead and Sin, God Will Forgive You (Yom Kippur Is Coming)
Counter-Argument: Do Not Trust the Yetzer Hara; Trust in God's Judgment
The Question: Who Will Know If I Sin in Secret? (Refutation: The Bricks and Beams of Your House Will Testify)
The Soul Testifies Against You; The Two Angels Accompanying You Testify; Your Limbs Testify
Real-World Application: How Actions Reflect on Character, Environment, and the Body; Concluding Blessing for Rosh Hashanah
